data.title: Sportradar Group AG: Both The Moat And Growth Outlook Have Improved
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on Sportradar Group AG (SRAD) was a buy rating because I was confident in the growth ahead as the drivers were visible. I also believed SRAD had strong pricing power. As for this update, I am staying bullish on SRAD because it now has more ways to monetize, and its moat has gotten stronger. IMG strengthens the platform, AI adds product and margin upside, and Playradar opens another growth driver.

IMG

Relative to my initiation coverage, I think the biggest development in the thesis is IMG. As a quick recap, my view on SRAD’s moat is that it sits in the middle of the ecosystem, has deep integrations, and benefits from operators not wanting to manage a disparate set of vendors (for data, odds, streaming, and trading tools). The IMG acquisition makes SRAD’s moat a lot stronger. For context, the acquired portfolio includes relationships with >70 rights holders, ~38,000 official data events, and ~29,000 streaming events across 14 global sports on six continents. That is a massive boost to what SRAD already has. Post the deal, SRAD’s sports coverage will increase to >1 million matches annually, and the benefits are obvious. Now, SRAD has more data to sell, more streaming to distribute, and more inventory to work with. But I think an important point is how management integrates IMG into the existing product set. Specifically, they said IMG content is being integrated into products such as Foresight, micro markets, player props, and the Virtual Live Match Tracker. Which means IMG is not operated as a standalone side product; it is being well integrated so that clients will not face the friction of “adopting another product" and at the same time reap the benefits of this IMG acquisition, which ultimately gives SRAD justification to step up monetization.

The rate at which IMG is being “monetized” is also very positive. Management said the majority of clients, including all Tier 1 partners, have already signed on for IMG data, odds, and audiovisual products. They also said SRAD is on track to unlock revenue synergies of 25% for IMG in 2026, which tells us how well IMG fits the platform. That is why I think IMG is such an important development in the thesis. SRAD’s current moat is so much stronger and deeper, such that it makes the platform harder to replicate and more valuable to existing customers.

AI

A new narrative that emerged is AI, which I think is beneficial for SRAD. I think SRAD benefits in three ways. First, it improves data collection. Management said historically ~50% of its data has been collected through computer vision, and with AI, that is moving toward 75% to 80% over time. Second, it helps SRAD to create better new products. For example, the basketball foundation model is able to predict what is likely to happen next in a game, which makes richer betting and engagement tools possible. Third, it can also lower internal costs by helping to handle large volumes of data requests and supporting back-end functions.

A product example here is the customized 4Sight product for NBC Universal Peacock’s NBA coverage. This is an AI-powered live-streaming product that overlays real-time stats, visual cues, and actionable insights directly onto live video. This effectively turns a standard stream into a more interactive viewing experience. Take the case of basketball: 4Sight will process a very large volume of official data to generate animated overlays and insights that can help predict likely outcomes in a game, which in turn can support richer fan engagement and better real-time betting decisions. In other words, this is a customer-facing product layer that can make SRAD’s content more valuable and more monetizable. Management even said that many of the underlying costs are already locked in, which means the incremental margins could be massive once SRAD starts ramping up these AI products.

iGaming

At the March conference, management launched Playradar as a dedicated iGaming brand. The idea is to connect sportsbook and casino experiences through hybrid content (including virtual sports and casino products such as slots, table games, arcade games, and crash games). First of all, I think this is a move that carries very little risk. SRAD is not entering this market from scratch. SRAD already works with 800 sportsbooks; 70% to 80% of those partners also have an iGaming component, and it already delivers 500,000 video streams annually to sportsbook partners. This means SRAD already has an installed base, distribution, and content capabilities. Secondly, the TAM is huge. Management sized the iGaming market at >$100 billion and said it does not need to spend heavily upfront or hurt margins to pursue the opportunity. We still have to see how well SRAD can execute here, but if it goes well, this could become a huge growth driver.

Valuation

The upside is even more attractive today than the last time I wrote about this. Both FY25 revenue growth and adj. EBITDA margin came in better than I expected, and management FY26 guidance is very bullish, calling for 22% y/y revenue growth and adj. EBITDA margin expanding to 25%. With this, I am upgrading my growth assumption from mid-teens to 20% for FY27 and FY28. I keep my adj. EBITDA is the same at 26%, which translates to adj. EBITDA growth of >20%. If SRAD does as I expect, I believe the current 10x forward EBITDA multiple is too low relative to history. I still think SRAD deserves to trade back to its historical average of 18x since its adj. EBITDA growth is still strong. It is unlikely we see SRAD trade back to 25x, like it did in early-to-mid 2025 when adj. EBITDA growth was >30%. But at >20%, I think 18x is a fair multiple.

Risk

I think the key risk now is execution. There are now a couple of key moving parts in the business: integrating IMG, pushing into iGaming, and trying to monetize newer products like 4Sight. I am hopeful of how they would play out, but if those revenue streams scale slower than expected while sports rights costs keep rising, margin expansion could disappoint.

Conclusion

I reiterate my buy rating for SRAD. The moat is getting stronger, and there are more ways to grow now. IMG should deepen customer value, AI can lift monetization and margins, and Playradar adds another leg of growth with limited upfront risk. If management executes well from here, I think the current valuation still looks too low for a business that can grow adj. EBITDA at >20% over the next few years.

data.title: Cadre Holdings: Nuclear Stock Meltdown Creates Upgraded Strong Buy Opportunity
data.description:
Cadre Holdings (CDRE) stock has tumbled nearly 25% since my last report, substantially underperforming the S&P 500. The price return is extremely disappointing for a company on which I have a buy rating and with exposure to the law enforcement and nuclear markets. Cadre Holdings has ventured into the nuclear markets through acquisitions, but we are now also seeing that the nuclear market also holds its own set of headwinds apart from the positive growth characteristics.

In this report, I discuss the company’s Q4 2025 and explain why I believe the stock is now a strong buy.

Cadre Holdings Investor Day Lacks Useful Detail

Cadre Holdings posted a 5% decline in net sales, which was definitely disappointing, as analysts had anticipated around 4% growth. Gross margins slipped 50 basis points, while net income declined from $13 million to $11.7 million or from $0.32 per share to $0.27 per share. Adjusted EBITDA declined nearly 11% to $34.4 million, driven by the 5% decline in sales and a 1.3 point decline in adjusted EBITDA margins.

While I do like the positioning of Cadre as a company with exposure to the law enforcement and nuclear markets, in my view they have always fallen short in providing useful insights into the growth drivers. Especially when markets are in a risk-off mode and financial results fall behind expectations, it can cause significant pressure on the share price performance.

The positive was that the reason for the miss was primarily due to contracts shifting from 2025 into 2026. So it is not a loss of contracts but the timing of contracts. However, there is also a more structural issue, and that is that the US policy is shifting away from plutonium downblending, and that reduces demand for the products of Alpha Safety, which the company acquired in 2024 and is a high-margin business for Cadre Holdings.

Cadre Holdings acquired Alpha Safety for $106.5 million in 2024 funded with cash on hand and debt. With the business coming from Alpha Safety now being softer than initially expected, I do believe that investors are wondering whether Cadre Holdings didn’t pay too much for Alpha Safety. Especially for Cadre Holdings, which sees little low-to mid-single-digit organic growth supplemented by $100 million in annual M&A, the possible fumble on Alpha Safety may have investors take a more cautious stance.

Cadre Holdings Aims For Strong Growth In 2026

For 2026, Cadre Holdings is targeting 22.4% growth in sales at the midpoint of the $736-$758 million range, with 24% growth in adjusted EBITDA with a guided range of $136-$141 million. That points at margins of around 18.5%. Organic growth is set to accelerate from 2% in 2025 to 3%-5%. The main growth contributor is the acquisition of TYR Tactical, which has an annual run rate of $100 million with an expected contribution this year of high $80 million to low $90 million at margins of 20%. The acquisition of TYR Tactical was finalized in February this year for $120 million in cash, $24 million in stock and $1 million restricted stock units with an additional $25 million earn-out consideration. TYR Tactical provides military gear, plate carriers and tactical equipment, making it a fit for Cadre’s law enforcement business.

Cadre Holdings Stock Upgraded To Strong Buy

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Cadre Holdings, I believe that there is 61% upside to $49.27 based on forward earnings and even 88% when valuing the stock another year ahead. The stock remains undervalued against peers, which trade at an EV/EBITDA of nearly 21x. Sales growth is expected to be 12.2% annually through 2028, with 17.7% annual growth in EBITDA and 23.1% annual growth in free cash flow, while also modeling in around $100 million in acquisitions. The challenging part is that I already modelled in this M&A activity, but the exact inorganic upside for revenues, EBITDA and FCF depends on what company will be acquired and the closing data of the acquisitions. Nonetheless, I do believe that the recent stock price decline offers a compelling entry point, and as a result, I am upgrading the stock to strong buy with a $49.27 price target as the base case, providing 61% upside, and $55.22 in a more bullish scenario, providing 80% upside.

The expectation is that in the years ahead, operating leverage will increase through a combination of organic and inorganic growth, pushing EBITA margins north of 21%. We also note that free cash flow is expected to significantly increase, and while I do believe that the company will continue to fund M&A through a combination of debt and cash on hand, it will also be better positioned to fund more of its acquisitions through cash flows. We do note that the company has $260.4 million in debt maturing in 2029 (10K, Page 71), which it could technically cover through expected free cash flow for 2029 in combination with cash on hand, but I do believe that the company will refinance some of its debt, if not all. One risk that remains is that some acquisitions are financed with stock.

Conclusion: Cadre Holdings Is Looking More Attractive

Cadre Holdings stock substantially declined due to fourth-quarter revenues slipping into 2026, with plutonium downblending demand seemingly facing a more structural demand pressure. However, I do believe that the stock price decline is somewhat overdone, offering an attractive entry point for investors interested in a company that is highly active in the M&A space. As a result, I am upgrading the stock to strong buy.

data.title: Sigma Lithium: Turning Into A Cash Machine
data.description:
Sigma Lithium Corporation (SGML) got a big boost this week after the lithium miner altered the cash flow picture. The company is benefitting from higher lithium prices (LC:COM) and an ongoing realization that growth drivers remain strong despite ongoing weakness in the U.S. EV market. My investment thesis is Bullish on the stock due to the strong expansion plans without the need to raise additional capital.

Major Offtake Agreements

Sigma Lithium produces the Quintuple Zero lithium with zero drinking water, dirty power, or hazardous chemicals used in the process to produce lithium. The biggest question with the business has been the cash position, especially last year when lithium prices got so low.

The lithium miner has now signed multiple volume commitments with $146 million in pre-payments for offtake agreements to solve the financial picture. Sigma Lithium is now on a path to a much stronger future after ending 2025 with a cash balance of only $6 million.

The company forecasts Q1 '26 (the quarter that just ended) cash inflows of $35 million, followed by a large $96 million payment in Q2. The company announced the major $96 million prepayment for 70,500 tons of production in 2026.

Sigma Lithium plans another offtake agreement for 80K tons annually with a $100 million prepayment. The company plans to use this prepayment to repay some of the long-term debt currently outstanding at $140 million.

Expansion Plans

Over the course of the next few years, Sigma plans to expand the production volumes by expanding from 1 plant to 3 plants. The goal is for annual lithium production to triple from 240,000 tpa to 770,000 tpa in 2028.

Based on forecasted costs dipping to $495/t, down from $592/t now, with $60/t assigned to interest expense reductions, Sigma Lithium projects the potential to produce $900 million in operating cash flows based on nameplate capacity goals for 2028 with lithium prices at $2,000/t.

The company repaying debt via the prepayments helps reduce a good portion of the cost reductions planned over the next couple of years. Sigma Lithium plans scale and operating efficiencies to help reduce the additional costs.

The lithium miner is only forecasting $80 million to build Plant 2 to boost production volumes to 520,000/t in 2027 and another $100 million for Plant 3 to further increase production to 770,000/t in 2028. Sigma Lithium only needs to spend $180 million total on capex, with the potential for annual cash flows to far exceed the amount of money spent to build these plants. For this reason, management doesn't plan to raise any additional capital despite a low cash balance to start the year.

The stock currently has a market cap of just $1.3 billion. If one has a positive view on lithium prices, Sigma Lithium could be producing cash flows closer to the market cap in a few years, and the company used the Q4 '25 earnings call to point out this discrepancy.

The company is currently forecasting quarterly sales in the range of $48 million, but naturally lithium prices play a huge part in actual sales totals. The consensus analyst estimates forecast revenues reaching $500 million in 2027, which, of course, appears very conservative considering Sigma Lithium forecasts cash flows of up to $500 million based on current lithium price realizations around the $1,800/t level.

The stock has limited analyst coverage, so the opportunity is for additional coverage to boost the visibility of Sigma Lithium. As with all lithium producers, the downside risk is lower lithium prices, with the stock trading at $5 just months ago.

The ultimate risk is for global use of lithium to not grow while these additional supplies come online. The EV battery and battery storage markets could always develop a better battery technology that replaces lithium.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Sigma Lithium still appears far too cheap with the projected cash flows. The stock traded up above $40 back in 2023 when lithium prices were much higher, and the opportunity exists to return to such levels on higher lithium production without the need for higher prices.

Investors should continue buying Sigma Lithium before the cash flow picture is fully understood.

data.title: I'm Not Buying Tuesday's 'Hopium' Rally
data.description:
Equities staged a rip-your-face-off rally on Tuesday to end a dismal first quarter for investors on a high note. The NASDAQ led the advance with better than a 3.8% rise, the index's best daily performance since May of last year. The small-cap Russell 2000 was up better than 3.4%, and the Dow and S&P 500 climbed more than 2% on the day. Hopes for a cessation of hostilities in the Middle East were the impetus for the rally.

Interestingly, WTI oil rose a bit over a buck a barrel Tuesday despite rising hopes the effective closure in the Strait of Hormuz would be lifted. Brent crude did fall over 4% on the day. To be fair, both WTI and Brent oil prices are falling some early Wednesday. In addition, the POTUS is scheduled to address the country later today about this conflict. One has to assume the administration would not have booked airtime if there wasn't good news to report.

Obviously, an ending to the hostilities in the Middle East and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz will trigger a huge sigh of relief from global investors. A further rally in the equity markets is also highly likely. However, I will not be buying this rally. I will use the rebound in equities to "roll" some of the options on the covered call positions in my portfolio that don't look like they will expire in the money from April to June to pick up additional premiums. Other than that, my trading action will be limited. My portfolio will remain roughly 30% in short-term Treasuries and cash and 70% within covered call holdings around the few stocks in the market I am finding with reasonable valuations.

And that is because I am not convinced that we are nearly out of the woods on several fronts. First, even if the Strait of Hormuz opened fully by the end of the week, it will take weeks for smelters, refineries, and chemical plants in the region to restart fully. In addition, many assets in the region were damaged or destroyed completely and will take months and years to bring back online. A massive Qatar LNG facility took some significant damage that could take five years to fully restore.

And even if oil, LNG, phosphate, urea, nitrogen, aluminum, etc. start to flow freely through this global chokepoint, prices for these products will remain significantly higher than they were before this conflict for many months, if not throughout the rest of 2026. Large oil reserves were released in Europe and the United States, and they will need to be replenished over time. Other commodities will need to be restocked, and some production will be lost forever. In addition, insurance rates for tankers and carriers traversing the Strait of Hormuz are likely to be much higher than they were before this regional war.

As I noted the other day, residential real estate is off to a rough start to 2026 after three years of moribund existing home sales. January new home sales showed a large drop, and average mortgage rates remain above those of September 2024, when the Fed started to take down the Fed Funds rate by a cumulative 175bps to date. Home sellers outnumber home buyers by the largest percentage since Redfin started to keep records in 2013.

Commercial real estate, or CRE, is in even worse shape. Trepp came out Tuesday with their March look at Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities, or CMBS. The overall CMBS delinquency rate for March rose 41bps month-over-month to 7.55%. The CMBS delinquency rate for office properties (some $1.3 trillion in debt outstanding) moved up to nearly 12% in March. This is considerably higher than the peak in the Great Financial Crisis, it should be noted. Roughly $600 billion in office-based CRE needs to be refinanced in 2026 and 2027. Office values have collapsed in many major cities thanks to factors like the explosion in the virtual workforce following Covid.

CMBS delinquency rates also surged above 7% for lodging and multi-family properties. The latter sector is quite important, as there is some $2.2 trillion of debt outstanding. Apartment vacancy rates are at their highest levels since records started to be tracked on this metric, and national rents have fallen nationally over six of the last eight months. Of note, the CMBS delinquency rates for all three of these sectors were under 2% before Chairman Powell started to lift the Fed Funds rate in March 2022.

Then, we have the deteriorating situation in private credit, where a new "cockroach" seemed to emerge every few days in the month of March. Many major private credit funds "gated" redemptions in March, including Apollo Global Management (APO), Cliffwater, Morgan Stanley (MS), and BlackRock (BLK). JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) marked down the collateral used to lend money to the private credit sector in March, which sent a shot across the bow in this space, as I noted in this recent article.

The consumer was struggling before the recent spike in gasoline and diesel prices in March thanks to the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz. With revisions, job growth only averaged 15,000 jobs a month in 2025. February saw a 92,000-job loss, according to the BLS. Oracle (ORCL) is one of several companies laying off a significant percentage of their workforce because of AI, joining other notable names like Block (XYZ) and Meta Platforms (META).

Delinquencies across the roughly $4.5 trillion of combined debt that consumers hold in student, credit card, and auto loans have been on the rise. One new factoid came to my attention this week that highlights the challenging conditions consumers face. 30.5% of buyers trading in a car in March toward a new vehicle are carrying negative equity. This is according to a JD Power report that came out last week. This metric has seen a steady rise since the Federal Reserve started to tighten monetary policy in the first quarter of 2022. This is up 4.2% from the same period in 2025, of note. In addition, consumers were on track to purchase $49.6 billion worth of new vehicles in March. This is down nearly 14% from March 2025. The average cost of a new vehicle in March was north of $49,000.

Valuations and breadth in the market are hardly compelling even after equities decline in the first quarter. The Shiller PE ratio has come down some since the first of the year due to losses in the stock market year to date. However, it remains substantially elevated at 37.6x. To put it in perspective, this is roughly 10 points higher than this metric was in 2007, prior to the subprime crisis unfolding.

Finally, while the three major indexes are no longer in correction territory after Tuesday's big rally, they are covering for weakness throughout the market. At the beginning of the week, according to Morgan Stanley, some 42% of S&P 500 members were already down 20% or more from their 52-week highs. Not exactly encouraging breadth when over 40% of the S&P 500 are experiencing bear markets.

Therefore, I view the huge rise in equities on Tuesday as a "hopium" rally. I remain cautious on the overall market given the issues on myriad fronts as well as extreme valuation levels and discouraging breadth.

data.title: Freshworks: Value Is Becoming Difficult To Ignore (Upgrade)
data.description:
We've completed a bruising first quarter of 2026, and so far, the enterprise software sector remains at historic lows, dogged by a "SaaSpocalypse" narrative that continues to persist despite relatively strong results by many companies in the space. While I think we're not likely to see any near-term recovery in multiples, long-term-oriented investors have a fantastic opportunity to buy many stocks here at a deep discount.

Companies like Freshworks (FRSH), in particular, have become quite compelling buys against bottom-line metrics. While I can't argue with the fact that a tough macro is putting pressure against growth rates, we have to ask: How much of that risk is already priced in, after Freshworks' ~30% decline since the start of the year?

I last wrote a "Neutral" article on Freshworks in November, when the stock was trading at $12 per share. Since then, Freshworks has seen its share price erode by roughly one-third. We won't disagree that execution has been a sticky point recently, with the company shedding total customers (though large customer counts continue to grow). At the same time, we're also observing meaningful bottom-line expansion, which reduces our reliance on revenue multiples to justify Freshworks' share price. With all of these factors weighed, I'm raising my rating on the stock to a "Buy."

In my view, these are the top reasons to be long on Freshworks:

  • Complex software that is largely AI-resistant. As a reminder, Freshworks' core products automate customer service, employee/customer experience, and sales/CRM tools. Each of these products is integrated into companies' daily operations, with integrations into third-party systems that make ripping out Freshworks and replacing it with AI solutions difficult. The company's recent buildup in large customers suggests that more customers are viewing the space as better to "buy" than to "DIY build."
  • Upmarket push and reduced reliance on smaller customers. Freshworks has been landing larger clients, especially with its Employee Experience (EX) software suite. The company has greater potential to cross-sell into larger companies. Over 60% of the company's ARR is generated from enterprise and mid-market clients, which gives the company revenue stability amid macroeconomic downturns.
  • Growth/profitability balance. Though not quite a "Rule of 40" software company, we note that Freshworks balances mid-teens revenue growth against a near-20% pro forma operating margin, still positioning Freshworks above a "Rule of 30" as it works to improve its bottom-line margins.
  • Strong balance sheet. Freshworks has nearly $0.8 billion of cash on its balance sheet, unencumbered by debt. This represents a large chunk of the stock's current market cap, enabling buybacks and potential growth acquisitions.

The top reason to "Buy" this stock remains valuation. At current share prices just above $8, Freshworks trades at a market cap of $2.27 billion. Netting off the $781.4 million of cash on the company's latest balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $1.49 billion.

For FY26, as shown in the chart below, Freshworks is guiding to $952-$960 million in revenue, or 14% midpoint growth. If we assume FCF grows in line with revenue at 14% y/y (and at a ~27% adjusted FCF margin), FY26 FCF would be $254.3 million.

This positions Freshworks' valuation at:

  • 1.6x EV/FY26 revenue
  • 5.9x EV/FY26 FCF
  • 14.5x FY26 P/E, or 9.5x ex-cash P/E

These are appealing bottom-line multiples. While we do note that Freshworks has issues in sales execution as well as dilution (stock comp of $147 million in FY25 made up roughly two-thirds of the year's cash flow), we think the company's combination of recurring revenue, upmarket customer push, and improving profitability make this a compelling long-term investment.

Stay long here and use this year's dip as a buying opportunity.

Q4 download

Let's now go through Freshworks' latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Freshworks' revenue grew 14% y/y to $222.7 million, slightly ahead of Wall Street's expectations of $218.7 million (+12% y/y). Revenue growth decelerated slightly relative to 15% y/y growth in Q3.

One of the core issues with Freshworks' recent performance has been sluggish customer growth. As shown below, the company's y/y growth in customer counts slowed to just 3% y/y growth, while total customers of 74.5k shed ~400 customers relative to 74.9k as of the end of Q3.

That said, Freshworks has been intent on landing larger deals recently, and it's deprioritizing its smaller SMB clients. Though the company lost customers as a whole, the client segment of customers who generate more than >$50k in ARR grew by 148 sequentially to 3,760 such customers, up 23% y/y.

Speaking further to Freshworks' upmarket push on the Q4 earnings call, CEO Dennis Woodside commented as follows:

We saw an upmarket momentum surge with our enterprise cohorts outpacing overall growth, proving our ability to consistently win and scale within the world's most complex organizations. As of Q4, we now have over 1,500 customers with greater than $100,000 in ARR, an increase of 28% year-over-year and over 3,700 customers with greater than $50,000 in ARR, an increase of 23% year-over-year. For our first strategic priority in employee experience, we crossed the $0.5 billion milestone as of the end of 2025, reaching $510 million in ARR. That represents 26% year-over-year growth on an as-reported basis and 22% year-over-year on a constant currency basis.

Now we are witnessing a generational shift where midsized and larger enterprise organizations expect sophisticated software that can handle their complex needs and get fast time to value. Freshservice is uniquely positioned to fill this gap left by legacy providers like ServiceNow.

We like the fact that Freshworks is remaining in net expansion territory, with net dollar retention at 108% in the quarter (with some benefit from FX; on a constant-currency basis, net retention was 104%, indicating the average customer boosted their spending on the Freshworks platform by 4% y/y).

We are wary of the fact that the company has been spending a good deal more on sales and marketing lately. Pro forma sales and marketing costs, excluding stock comp, rose to 43% of revenue in Q4, up from 39% in the year-ago quarter. This had the impact of blunting operating margins, down to 18.7% in Q4 (-200bps y/y).

That said, we do note that the company is intent on fixing its go-to-market issues. In March, the company announced a streamlined global sales org, letting go of its "chief customer growth officer" and unifying sales leadership under a new Chief Revenue Officer position. We're hopeful that the company's intent to address sales performance will lead to positive deal momentum in FY26.

Free cash flow in Q4 rose 35% y/y to $56.2 million. On a full-year basis, the company generated $223.1 million in free cash flow, up 46% y/y and hitting a 26.6% FCF margin that improved 530bps y/y.

Risks and key takeaways

Of course, there are also risks at play here that we need to keep in mind. As previously mentioned, many investors are wary of Freshworks' stock comp, which at $147 million in FY25 constituted the majority of the company's free cash flow. That said, there is a self-regulating mechanism here in Freshworks' declining share prices; we expect RSU values to diminish and many employee options to expire without value due to the large declines in Freshworks' share price since the start of the year, which should reduce dilution in FY26. We note as well that the recent sales leadership transition could drive near-term friction in deal momentum.

We do find that these risks are fairly priced into the stock, especially with Freshworks trading at a <10x ex-cash P/E multiple. Though we'll likely need to be patient to achieve a true upward multiples re-rating, the stock is now low enough to take a stake.

data.title: Coca-Cola Consolidated: Great Compounder, But A Little Stretched (Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

It’s been over a year since the last time I covered Coca-Cola Consolidated, Inc. (COKE), so there is no better time than revisiting after the company announced its full-year ’25 results. Since the article back in January of 2025, when I maintained my Buy rating, the share price was very resilient and advanced over 45% compared to SPY’s (SPY) 9%.

At this point, I feel like COKE’s valuation is a little stretched, and I would be looking to add to your position on any weakness that we may get in the future. I still believe it will be a great compounder, but I am not a buyer at these levels.

Briefly on Performance

Starting from the top-line performance over the last year, we can see that the bottler kept it quite steady. Full-year ’25 revenues came in at around $7.2B, up around 5%, with over 99% of the revenues coming from the nonalcoholic beverages.

Sure, the growth isn’t impressive, but it never was with the bottler in general. It is a steady compounder in my book, and that is quite attractive to some investors.

On the profitability front, we can see some issues cropping up, especially in the last quarter of the year. We can see that gross margins took a hit. Are we surprised? Not at all. The cost of aluminum increased slightly, and the management put the blame on elevated import tariffs. The decline wasn’t significant, but it certainly has affected its overall performance.

Looking at other key efficiency and profitability metrics, we can see that the end of the year was good for COKE. Everything went up, meaning the management utilized the company’s assets and shareholder capital rather efficiently, thus creating value. Additionally, COKE boasts quite a high return on total capital, or ROTC, which tells me the company’s moat and pricing power are still in place. That is what I would pay a premium for.

Let’s take a look at the company’s financial situation. At the end of the year, COKE had around $282m in cash and equivalents, against $2.7B in long-term debt. Is this worrisome? Not at all. The company made close to $1B in operating income, while it only paid out around $43m in interest expenses, which means its interest coverage ratio is over 22x. This is much higher than what many analysts say is healthy (2x) and even higher than what I like to see (5x). Additionally, COKE’s cash from operations was close to $932m, while its free cash flow was around $600m. The company is a cash machine. The debt on its books is not an issue, and its debt obligations are easily covered by its operations.

Overall, the company has performed as well as it has in the past. Nothing too exciting, and not terrible. It’s not a 10%+ growth compounder but more of a steady, low-mid-single-digit compounder who continues to perform well in any environment. It’s a very resilient business even as tariffs hit its operations.

Comments on the Outlook

Guidance for 2026

For this year, the company expects to continue its momentum of mid-single-digit growth across the board. Revenue is projected to be in the range of 4-5% organically, while also guiding 7-8% growth in comparable EPS. Nothing spectacular, as I said, but it’s also not bad either. I don’t expect to see a lot of changes in how the company is operating. There is constantly enough demand to keep putting up the same numbers year after year. It’s quite a resilient business.

Other developments

I think one of the biggest developments of the past year was that COKE bought out the Coca-Cola Company’s (KO) ownership stake. KO is no longer a shareholder in COKE, making it a lot more independent, which also required a massive outlay of cash and new debt financing. It is also quite weird that the Coca-Cola company has nothing to do with COKE any longer. But I think this is for the best for COKE. It will always be associated with KO, since it has to distribute Coca-Cola-branded beverages.

However, this move makes it more independent, giving the management more control over strategy and capital allocation. This I like to hear, and I wouldn’t be surprised to see the management working hard on deleveraging COKE’s balance sheet after that massive ownership buyout.

Another positive I see from this major development is the overall per-share economics. When the company did the buyout, the management retired a large block of the shares, which means future profits are going to be spread over fewer shares, helping EPS if the business maintains this momentum. Less splitting of the pie for all the shareholders is always a good thing. I suppose only time will tell if this independence truly is a positive, but I think more often than not, it is shareholder-friendly.

Unfortunately, a negative, if even, is that the company lowered its share repurchasing program from the original $1B to $400m, and it had around $136.3m left to repurchase. This also makes sense since the company just used up a lot of capital that was previously available for this shareholder-favored strategy. However, it is not that bad since the company already retired a big chunk of shares due to the buyout. A lot more than the original $1B repurchase program.

It also seems that the company may be switching gears to other beverages, specifically to Still beverages. Q4 ’25 showed that the Still category volume increased by almost 9%, compared to around 3.5% growth for the Sparkling volume. Due to this performance in the Still category, the management is actively shifting its portfolio to better align with what the consumer wants. And now it can do that since it has all the control. It wasn’t a forced move at all, I don’t think. The management saw that the Still beverages are doing much better, so the natural thing to do is to follow the money.

I think whatever strategy the management chases will work out in the end. I think the business model is very resilient and very well diversified, so if the management over-tweaks the strategy, the loss in sales or profitability will be very little. There’s not much it can do to grow at a much faster speed, and most definitely, it cannot do too much damage to its operations.

Valuation

Let’s take a look at a quick valuation model, since it has been a while since the last time I did one. Below are the assumptions I used.

I kept it simple: revenues are going to continue to grow at their usual growth rate, and on the lower end. Operating margin will continue to increase, but I kept it at a low increase as well to keep up with the guidance of around 7% growth. I think it can do better, but for now, I am being more conservative. The rest I am keeping static at the company’s historical averages. Additionally, I am using the company’s WACC of around 7.2% as my discount rate and 2.5% as my terminal growth rate. With these assumptions, we get the following projections.

And with these projections, we get COKE’s fair value to be around $185 a share, which means it is trading essentially at its intrinsic value for now.

Closing Comments

So, I think I would like to see if the company can improve its top-line growth rate going forward or faster margin improvement. Ideally, both, if I were to keep my buy rating. I think at these levels, COKE is a little stretched for my liking; therefore, I am downgrading it to a hold until we see how the company does over the next few quarters and completely independent from KO. I have no doubt it will continue to perform well, but I also believe that there will be a better time to add to your position. So, I suggest holding onto your COKE shares and adding on further weakness of the share price.

data.title: Lamb Weston: Sub-$40 Value Play With An EPS Rebound Expected
data.description:
In today’s column, we hone in on Lamb Weston Holdings, Inc. (LW), which is a big player in the global food industry. Recall that this company specifically focuses on the production and distribution of frozen potato products. It is a potato giant, as we often say, and years ago was a key asset within Conagra Brands (CAG). Of course, the company was spun off as an independent entity to better focus on its specialized commercial operations.

Today, Lamb Weston serves a massive array of food service companies, ranging from global fast-food giants to local restaurants, as well as retail channels. The company has navigated a complex landscape over the last year, characterized by shifting consumer habits and inflationary pressures that have impacted margins. To combat these headwinds, management has been aggressively executing its so-called Focus to Win strategy, which is designed to streamline operations, reduce structural capital intensity, and drive long-term efficiency. Recent strategic moves include closing older facilities in Argentina to consolidate production into more modern hubs and curtailing production lines in Europe to better align supply with a softer international demand environment. However, LW stock has just continued to struggle badly.

Despite a horrific three-year chart as shown above, we are seeing an interesting setup here on valuation and the potential for a return to growth. In fact, after this fiscal year ending May 2026, it is widely expected there will be a meaningful return to double-digit EPS growth:

In this column, we check in on the just-reported fiscal Q3 earnings. Let us discuss.

Mixed results for Lamb Weston in Q3

We have been monitoring what we view as a turnaround story at Lamb Weston for several quarters now, and the fiscal third-quarter results just released are a bit of a mixed bag. On one hand, we see the North America segment performing pretty well, but on the other, the International business is clearly feeling the heat from a challenging global macro environment. Revenues for the quarter came in at $1.56 billion, which represents 3% growth year-over-year. However, if you look under the hood and strip out a $47.4 million favorable currency impact, sales were essentially flat.

The story here is one of volume versus price, in our opinion. Volumes are good. We saw a 7% increase in volume, which is a great sign of customer retention and new contract wins in North America. But that was completely offset by a 7% decline in price/mix as the company used trade support and pricing modifications to keep those customers and respond to consumers shifting toward value-oriented channels.

There is no real way to sugarcoat it. The bottom-line earnings numbers took a significant hit this quarter, though that was expected largely. Net income plummeted 63% to $54 million, while adjusted EPS fell 37% to $0.72. When sales volumes come in lower than planned due to soft demand in places like the EMEA region, you end up with underutilized factories and wasted raw materials.

So what does that mean? It hurts EBITDA. We saw manufacturing costs higher, and adjusted EBITDA was down 27% to $271.7 million. Folks, management is doing what it can to shore up operations, such as closing the Munro plant in Argentina and slowing down lines in the Netherlands, because they know they have to get these costs under control to protect the earnings power of the business. This turnaround is a process. More work needs to be done internationally, but North America, as noted, looks quite good.

North America is strong; cost controls are needed

While the international headlines look rough, the North America segment is actually showing some impressive resilience. Net sales in the region grew 5% to over $1 billion. What really catches our eye is the 12% volume growth. In a market where many food companies are struggling to move units, Lamb Weston is winning share and securing contracts. There are a number of reasons why that may be, such as GLP-1s and people eating less or more healthily. Perhaps it is persistent inflation, higher utility costs, and higher gas prices, all weighing on consumers' ability to eat out.

But we still think the volumes are the story. Yes, they had to give up 7% in price/mix to get there, but building that volume base is critical for when margins eventually stabilize. We also saw improved operating efficiencies and lower manufacturing costs per pound in the domestic business, excluding the raw potato issues seen abroad. This suggests that the cost-savings initiatives are indeed bearing fruit where the company has the most control.

Looking at the balance sheet, the company remains in a solid position to weather this transition. They ended the quarter with roughly $1.3 billion in total liquidity. Even with the earnings pressure, they returned $51.4 million to shareholders via dividends this quarter. While they paused buybacks during the quarter due to trading restrictions, they have already been back in the market since those restrictions lifted, repurchasing over 1 million shares recently. This tells us management still views the shares as a good value here, and they are putting their money where their mouth is now that they have a 10b5-1 plan in place.

Looking ahead

Folks, the update to the fiscal 2026 guidance is where the rubber meets the road. Management narrowed the sales range to $6.45 billion to $6.55 billion and tightened the adjusted EBITDA target to $1.08 billion to $1.14 billion. Perhaps most importantly, they slashed their capital expenditure expectations by $100 million, down to approximately $400 million. To us, it’s a pretty clear signal that the heavy lifting of their major growth projects is finishing up, and they are now focused on harvesting cash flow rather than spending it. We are also encouraged to see that they now expect to exceed their original $250 million cost-reduction target by 2028. We think the company is turning around.

As we look ahead, we continue to think you should keep a close eye on the International segment for signs of stabilization. Further, Q4 will benefit from a 53rd week, which should provide a nice tailwind to the final annual numbers. We are looking for confirmation in the next few months that the supply-demand balance in Europe and Asia is normalizing.

If the company can maintain its volume momentum in North America and improve operational expenses, we may see a nice EPS inflection in fiscal 2027. For now, we view the stock as an interesting value play for patient investors, with a 3.7% dividend yield while you wait for a rebound in LW EPS next fiscal year.

data.title: Layoffs Closer To Drought Than Deluge: What 3 Sources Show About Jobs
data.description:
Job layoff announcements soared in 2025, up 58% from 2024. January’s figures were high, though February 2026 came in low, according to Challenger, Gray and Christmas. Unfortunately for those who watch the economy, the numbers are bogus. That’s not the fault of news media nor businesses that add up the announcements, such as Challenger. It’s the underlying layoff announcements that are false. The hard data shows the United States is closer to a layoff drought than a deluge.

The law provides a strong incentive to over-announce future layoffs. The WARN Act requires large companies (100 employees or more) to provide 60 days of notice when they are laying off 50 or more workers. (Details and exceptions are described by the Department of Labor.) Companies are subject to penalties if they do not warn employees of upcoming layoffs, but there are no penalties for announcing layoffs that don’t occur, nor for overstating layoffs. So, if a business is thinking about laying off about 100 workers, they may want to announce 120 or 150 people to be laid off. No penalty for too large an announcement, but penalties for too small an announcement. This measure also misses small employers, who are not subject to the WARN Act.

We have two alternative sources of information about layoffs. My favorite is initial claims for unemployment insurance. That figure, divided by total employment, tells us the risk that a person will be laid off. And it’s been trending down for years. The February risk of an employed person filing for unemployment insurance was 0.14%, compared to the average for the 1970s of 0.43%. And we are well below the average for 2024 and 2025.

The initial claims count is not perfect. It includes people who filed but were later denied benefits. That could be because they were fired for cause, had not been employed long enough, or were not available for work. Denied claims tend to be fairly stable year to year, but with a long, gradual downward trend that probably reflects less enforcement of the standards. The count may also miss people who are laid off and choose to retire or who find another job very quickly, plus a few who do not have enough time working to qualify for benefits. However, it does an excellent job of capturing the month-to-month trends, and the difference from true layoffs tends to be fairly stable over a short period of time.

Another measure that’s better than layoff announcements is from the Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey (JOLTS) from the Bureau of Labor Statistics. Their data show layoffs have been roughly stable since the summer of 2024. Recent years have also been lower than almost all of the period prior to the pandemic since the survey began in 2000.

Like all surveys, JOLTS is not perfect. The government seeks information from 21,000 employment establishments (which could be businesses, non-profits, or governments). That’s out of a total of 12 million establishments, so it’s a relatively small sample. It does, however, capture the ups and downs of layoffs.

Pulling together the three data sources, layoffs in recent years have been remarkably low. The very recent trend has been slightly positive, but not hugely so. But we work in a very different economy than years past. Layoffs make headlines more than they used to, but they impact a smaller portion of the workforce than in decades past. We can call it a layoff drought, so long as we don’t think of that as a bad thing.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Microsoft: A Gift At 22x P/E (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Buying MSFT stock at 22x P/E is a no brainer

I last wrote on Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) stock on Feb 20 in an article titled “Microsoft: Why I Set A $370 Buy Order.” That article was triggered by the changes in MSFT's implied volatility and Bollinger Bands at time. These changes have led me to see good odds for the share price to dip to $370 in the near future. And I further presented the following comments to argue why this price would be an excellent entry point for long-term investors:

Any P/E around 20x is a no-brainer for a stock with ROCE (return on capital employed) around 65% like MSFT in my experience. At $370, MSFT would be priced around 22x FWD based on its latest EPS projections. At this level of ROCE, a 10% reinvestment rate would provide 6.5% organic real growth rates (and that is before inflation escalator). And a ~22x P/E would at least provide 4.5% of earnings yield in MSFT’s case as its owners’ earnings yield consistently exceeds its EPS.

Since that writing, the share prices have indeed touched $370 (and is hovering around $371) at the time of this writing. Following the above argument, I thus see an annual return potential in the double digits for the long term (around 11% consisting of 4.5%+ earning yielding and 6.5% growth rate), motivating me to adjust my rating to Buy.

Besides the above valuation changes, another new catalyst that is on my radar is the company’s upcoming earnings report (ER) for FQ3 on April 23rd, which further supports the rating upgrade for reasons detailed in the next section.

MSFT stock: FQ3 earnings preview and Rule of 40

MSFT is expected to announce its next ER on April 23, and the ER will be for its FQ3 2026. As shown in the snapshot below, based on the latest consensus estimates, the company is projected to maintain strong profitability and growth. Analysts have set the EPS GAAP estimate at $4.09 on total revenue of $81.36B. These figures represent a year-over-year revenue growth of 15.1% and an implied net margin (on a GAAP basis) of 37.5%. Note that in my estimate of its FWD net margin, I assume the number of outstanding shares remains the same as in the past quarter.

Given Microsoft’s leading role as a provider of PaaS/IaaS solutions (it is one of the three public cloud providers that can deliver such solutions at scale in my view), I feel the so-called rule of 40 to be fitting frame to contextualize its above growth and profitability potential. For readers new to the rule of 40 (simply referred to as R40 or the rule hereafter), the tutorial below should provide sufficient background for the rest of the discussion.

Wall Street Prep: The management teams of SaaS companies are often required to choose between rapid growth and expansion, or improving profitability – for which, the Rule of 40 has become a practical framework to balance the two concepts. The Rule of 40 – popularized by Brad Feld – states that a SaaS company’s revenue growth rate plus profit margin should be equal to or exceed 40%. Thus, the Rule of 40 measures the balance between a SaaS company’s growth rate and profitability.

Under this framework, profitability can be measured by a variety of metrics ranging from EBITDA margin all the way to net profit margin (NPM). The further we go downstream of the income statement, the more stringent the rule becomes and the harder it is for a given company to pass. But in MSFT’s case, it’s very likely to pass given the above FQ3 outlook even in terms of its NPM. Based on the projections just mentioned, the sum of its expected growth of 15.1% and NPM of 37.5% is 52.6, far exceeds the passing score of 40.

To better contextualize the above figures, the next chart shows MSFT’s R40 score in the past. This chart again computed the R40 score by adding its YOY quarterly revenue growth to its GAAP NPM. As seen, MSFT has habitually surpassed the passing score in the long term, a strong demonstration of its superb balance of growth and profitability.

MSFT stock: valuation revisited

With the above R40 score, I’d like to revisit its valuation again and argue why the accounting P/E ratio mentioned earlier, as appealing as it is, could have still understated how attractive the current valuation is. As shown in the chart below, its FWD P/E ratio of ~22x is about 30% discounted from its historical average. But here I want to draw your attention to the EV/sales metric. On an FWD basis, MSFT is currently trading at about 8.5x EV/Sales ratio as seen.

Such an EV/sales ratio is unusually low in my view for a business that can exceed the R40 requirement by a wide margin. As a reflection of this view, the following statics provided by McKinsey helps to benchmark MSFT’s EV/sales multiple by other companies who can also pass the R40. According to this report:

Data show that investors reward companies that are at or above the Rule of 40 with consistently higher enterprise value (EV) to revenue multiples. Moreover, the higher the number, the greater the gain. Top-quartile SaaS companies generate nearly three times the multiples of those in the bottom (exhibit). As seen, for SaaS companies that meet or exceed the rule, the overall EV/sales multiple is 12x and the multiple is as high as 22x for those in the top quartile.

With these statistics, MSFT’s FWD EV/sales ratio is discounted from the overall average of 12x by almost 30% despite the fact that its R40 score has been and will likely to be far above the passing score.

Other risks and final thoughts

In terms of downside risks, the ongoing geopolitical conflicts have created and can continue creating near term volatility for MSFT share prices. But eventually, I consider such issues to be temporary only. A more secular uncertainty involves the role of AI for the software ecosystem. The capabilities from large language models (LLMs) are rapidly advancing, and there is certainly the possibility for LLMs to disrupt the existing business model of MSFT together with many other software companies.

Although MSFT is better prepared for such disruptions in my mind with its investment in OpenAI (OPENAI) and the integration of AI feature in its core products such as Office 365 and LinkedIn. In particular, Office 365 enjoys virtual monopoly in office productivity software per my assessment, and the subscription model and integration of AI feature provide plenty of up-sale opportunities for MSFT. These opportunities help to offset the potential negative disruptions perceived by the market from AI.

All told, this revisit to MSFT is primarily triggered by the two developments since my last writing: the valuation changes and also the FQ3 earnings outlook. Market volatility has compressed MSFT’s valuation to only 22x P/E, and simple math shows me how attractive this is for long-term holding. The attractiveness is further augmented by the forecast of its future earnings and profitability metrics. These forecasts point to a superb balance of growth and margins per the rule of 40, which makes the stock even more discounted when compared to other company that can also pass the rule.

data.title: What GLD Options Say About The Price Of Gold (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
We believe in the importance of investor sentiment and use option data in GLD plus investor surveys to forecast the price of GLD. These forecasts are for the intermediate-term, which are price moves that last from two to five months.

After the sharp selloff in gold, neither option data nor investor surveys are giving buy signals. Except for one metric called SKEW, everything points to a continuation of the correction that began last month.

Option Data

Since people buy “calls” when they expect higher prices and “puts” when they expect lower prices, option data provides powerful insight into investor expectations. This is not only true of stocks but gold as well.

The Basic “Puts To Calls” Ratio

Marty Zweig developed the puts-to-calls ratio over fifty years ago. He compared the buying of “put” and “call” contracts to decide if investors were bullish or bearish on the market. He found when “too many” bought calls, prices usually went down. When “too many” bought puts, prices usually rose. Their buying activity acted as a contrary opinion indicator.

While he compared contracts, we compared money, which we think is much more accurate.

The Premium “Puts To Calls” Ratio Of GLD

The graph below measures the puts-to-calls ratio of dollars flowing into directional puts versus calls in GLD. Again, we focus on dollars rather than contracts for greater accuracy. By applying a 20-day average, short-term fluctuations are smoothed into a more meaningful intermediate-term indicator.

The Red Zone Green Zone ranking scale compares ratios against historic norms. Green Zone readings mean an excessive amount of money is buying puts while Red Zone means an excessive amount of money is buying calls.

Green Zone signals of minus 9 or less are intermediate term buy signals while Red Zone signals of +9 or more are sell signals. Like with all contrary opinion indicators, buy signals are more reliable than sell signals.

With the sell off last month the ratio in GLD has moved away from the Red Zone readings at the end of the year toward the Green Zone. Our principle is that after a major signal the trend will continue until an extreme reading occurs the other way. As you can see the last Green Zone signal occurred in mid-2023.

We believe another major buy signal won't occur until we get another Green Zone signal, which occur when more money pours into the put side of the market. The current reading means we're not there yet. It could occur over the next month or two but at this time it's not there..

The Trade “Puts To Calls” Ratio Of GLD

To confirm the premium “puts to calls” ratio we also calculate the ratio of the number of put “trades” by call “trades.” This essentially measures how many people are buying puts compared to people buying calls. It helps us know if the premium ratio is being distorted by one or two large money trades. The two curves should parallel each other.

You can see that at the current time the two graphs look identical. This helps confirm that the premium puts to calls ratio accurately reflects investor sentiment and is not distorted by one or two excessively large money trades.

The SKEW

This chart shows GLD’s 20-day SKEW average. The SKEW measures the difference in implied volatility traders are giving 30 day, out of the money puts versus calls.

The implied volatility of puts and calls should be theoretically equal but they never are and the difference has meaning. The CBOE takes the difference, normalizes it and set that equal to zero. They then compare the difference with that. This is called SKEW.

We take the daily SKEW and average it over 20 days to achieve an intermediate-term perspective.

So a high number over zero means traders are overpricing out of the money puts and are therefore too bearish. This normally occurs at a market low.

Of the three option indicators, this is the one that is most bullish on GLD. It shows that Put buyers are aggressively buying puts at any price pushing up implied volatility.

Gold Advisor Survey

Mark Hulbert has been keeping track the number of advisors in gold who are bullish or bearish since before 2000. He does this every day. His data produces a wonderful contrary opinion indicator.

We take his daily numbers and form a time weighted moving average that we believe reflects the intermediate-term expectations of the advisors. We then position that moving average on our Red Zone Green Zone ranking scale. It's graph below.

Green Zone readings means too many advisors are bearish, which is a Green Zone buy signal. On the other hand a Red Zone reading means that too many advisors are bullish which is a bearish sign. Like with all contrary opinion indicators, buy signals are more reliable than sell signals.

After the large sudden sell off in March, the opinion of gold advisor on the price outlook is neutral. They are neither bullish nor bearish. According to our discipline, when an indicator is neutral you go into waiting mode. It’s our belief that investor sentiment almost always gives a signal before a major move starts.

Mixed Record

We have a mixed record on our GLD recommendations. We gave a long-term buy signal in GLD on August 26th, 2022 at a price of $163 (Investor activity in GLD suggests price breakout). That buy signal was pretty good since it was within a few percent of the all-time low of the last 10 years. The signal was based on contrary opinion indicators very similar to the information we're presenting in this article.

Then on March 11th 2025, we signaled the end of the three-year advance in GLD (GLD is headed for a measurable correction). This call was wrong and caused us to miss the next two years of price gains that GLD provided.

While we at the Sentiment King believe strongly in using investor sentiment to forecast price direction, this is a stark reminder that nothing is perfect.

That said we know of no other tool that is this as good over time at forecasting price moves over the intermediate term then measuring investor sentiment. In this case it got us in near the exact low but also got us out long before we were able to realize the full potential that GLD produced.

Summary

Both the ratio of money going into puts versus calls in GLD and the survey of gold advisors suggest that this gold decline of the last month is not over. They show that investors are not yet bearish enough to signal a major low.

The only option metric that is bullish at this time is the GLD SKEW, which compares implied volatility of puts versus calls in GLD. We don't believe it is as critical or as good at timing as the first two.

We follow these indicators on a daily basis and will notify Sentiment King subscribers as soon as we get a buy signal. We'll also write an article about it but out of necessity it will be a few weeks after the signal.
